vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only modifies sysctl configuration to set kernel.kptr_restrict, a standard hardening measure that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout. Applying the change at runtime and persisting it is low risk and unlikely to lock out access or break services.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Potential conflict with custom sysctl settings if existing entries are not properly commented; Incorrect value could reduce debugging capabilities

llm_verdict.message:
The kernel pointer exposure restriction has been remediated:

* **Persistent configuration** – the line in `/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf` was edited (line 68) so it now reads:
```
kernel.kptr_restrict = 2
```
* **Runtime configuration** – the setting was applied immediately with:
```
sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=2
```
The system now reports `kernel.kptr_restrict = 2` and the setting will survive a reboot, satisfying the **sysctl_kernel_kptr_restrict** rule.

review_feedback: Fix rejected by Review/QA. Suggestions: Add a check like `if [ -f /proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict ]; then ...` to ensure the parameter exists before writing.; After creating the file, run `restorecon -v /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf` to apply the correct SELinux label.; Instead of blanket commenting, evaluate each file and only modify those that are not part of the vendor package, or use a lower‑precedence drop‑in (e.g., 01‑…) if needed.; Consider reloading sysctl settings with `sysctl --system` or `sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf` after creation, rather than only `sysctl -w`.; Remove or archive the *.bak files after successful remediation to keep the filesystem tidy.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly sets kernel.kptr_restrict=2 at runtime and persists it in /etc/sysctl.d, handling existing entries. It is functional and low‑risk, though minor improvements are recommended.
concerns: Commenting out all existing kernel.kptr_restrict lines may unintentionally alter vendor‑provided configurations that rely on a different value.
The script does not explicitly set the SELinux context on the new file, which could cause it to be ignored on SELinux‑enforced systems.
No explicit check that the running kernel actually supports kernel.kptr_restrict before applying the value.
Backup files (.bak) are created but not cleaned up, potentially leaving clutter.
suggested_improvements: Add a check like `if [ -f /proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict ]; then ...` to ensure the parameter exists before writing.
After creating the file, run `restorecon -v /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf` to apply the correct SELinux label.
Instead of blanket commenting, evaluate each file and only modify those that are not part of the vendor package, or use a lower‑precedence drop‑in (e.g., 01‑…) if needed.
Consider reloading sysctl settings with `sysctl --system` or `sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-kptr-restrict.conf` after creation, rather than only `sysctl -w`.
Remove or archive the *.bak files after successful remediation to keep the filesystem tidy.
